Here we go again, the proportion of cases where a continuation was used after an abbreviation marker, like the example that raised this issue. These last numbers are pretty astonishing/sobering and indicate that most continuations possibly were a Bad Idea.

The first column measures the percentage of cases where the word is used after an abbreviation marker over its combined use after a sentence end marker (i.e., column 2 divided by column 2+3). In other words, the top words in this list are *excellent* continuation markers, the further down the list we get, the less likely so.

Likelih. | N. abbrev. | N. starters | Word
--- | --- | --- | ---
0.807 | 18950 | 4536 | was
0.732 | 2281 | 833 | into
0.713 | 11689 | 4699 | were
0.676 | 125059 | 60007 | and
0.556 | 6776 | 5406 | has
0.512 | 5887 | 5620 | or
0.358 | 439 | 786 | than
0.275 | 24804 | 65387 | is
0.257 | 11022 | 31855 | are
0.228 | 100770 | 341394 | of
0.191 | 1118 | 4740 | via
0.071 | 6242 | 81955 | that
0.057 | 18510 | 304337 | with
0.054 | 16472 | 290662 | from
0.048 | 2263 | 44610 | between
0.034 | 10653 | 303719 | by
0.026 | 1446 | 54675 | whether
0.018 | 105 | 5733 | nor
0.017 | 10446 | 616634 | at
0.016 | 9255 | 561735 | on
0.014 | 1445 | 100729 | within
0.012 | 1391 | 112907 | but
0.011 | 25779 | 2230428 | for
0.008 | 65529 | 7826016 | in
0.007 | 2105 | 279543 | during
0.007 | 12926 | 1793203 | to
0.005 | 6544 | 1365077 | as
0.004 | 3416 | 880313 | after
0.004 | 194 | 53599 | whereas
0.003 | 179 | 60536 | upon
0.001 | 558 | 424063 | while
0.001 | 32 | 51213 | though

I would say that everything below the 1% mark (in, during, to, as, after, whereas, upon, while, and through) are candidates that really *must* be removed from the continuation list, because they are hardly ever used inside a sentence after an abbreviation marker in comparison to its use after a sentence end marker.

This largely coincides/inverts with the lists we found earlier, and adds *in*, *to*, *as*, and *upon*. With the exception of *to*, all words are fine a priori, as they are quite high up in the other list(s), too. Regarding *to*, we have: 13k times used after an abbrevation, 1.8M times as a sentence starter, and 57M times inside sentences. According to the statistic, we will convert less than 1% of all "tos", i.e., those that were used as an abbreviation, into sentence starters if we remove the word from the list of continuations. So @Klim314, I hope you are happy - *to* made it off the list! :-)

What actually bothers me more is the long list of words between 10 and 1% (that - for). They are all at least 10 times more likely to be used at a sentence start than after an abbreviation. That means, they do not really warrant their use as continuation markers, at least according to this (pretty large) corpus. My judgement call for now is to comment them out from the code, but it would be nice if somebody could confirm this and run my `count_continuations.py` Python3 script on another corpus. Here is how to use the script, particularly if you have your corpus split over many small files:

```{bash}
find ~/work/corpora/pmc/* -name "*.txt" \
| xargs cat \
| ./count_continuations.py at after and are as at \
  between but by during for from has in into is \
  nor of on or than that though to upon via was \
  were whereas whether while with within \
| sort -rn
```

If you have only one large file or a few small ones, just pipe it into `count_continuations.py`.
I would really love to hear a second opinion from someone else, but for now, I will remove most continuations after observing these "sobering" numbers.

I'm sorry, but this doesn't make sense. Class attribute is not meant for storing defaults. When people look for public properties, they will look at the `__init__` function and see what are initialized there.

If you insist on your preference, that's fine, but I don't see consistency here. `connected` also has immutable default, why do you initialize it for each instance? Same thing for `passive` `peeraddress` `peerport` of `DCCConnection`

I'd very much like support for the project. I'd like to have a co-maintainer or even hand off the maintenance to someone with sufficient interest. I'd prefer to start that with some modest improvements that expand into more substantial changes. I despise forks (for the technical debt and confusion they add), and I'm willing to work with the community to make this library the best foundational library it can be and expose hooks in the right places to make it extensible.

This Python lib has been around for some time and has a fairly diverse user base, so any changes should be considerate of those users.

In a quick review of the revamp docs, I see some good ideas, but also some difficulties. Here's my first thoughts.

> Simplifying class chain SimpleIRCClient -> Reactor -> ServerConnection

The data model of the IRC client has always been a little clumsy. Getting that to be more simple and elegant (i.e. a "for humans" revamp) sounds great. Long ago, Reactor was called "IRC", which didn't add much value as a label. I briefly renamed it to Manifold before Reactor was suggested.

The three classes predate my adoption of the library, so I'm not certain what the motivations were of the separation. I'm happy for the separation if that's what provides users value to hook in at different points. It's possible that abstraction isn't necessary or can be achieved another way.

> Replacing process_once()/process_forever() with process(max_wait=..)

I know the Reactor was meant to be hookable by a number of event loops, allowing other event loops to be used. I believe the client was meant to be single-threaded, so switching to threads may have unintended consequences.

I'd be particularly interested to see how this change would affect a move to async io or fix something like #65.

> Delayed 001 / "welcome" event handler to wait for 004 and 005

This seems reasonable, though I imagine it could have issues for clients already configured to respond to 004 and 005. Is this the best way to respond, versus responding to each event in turn and augmenting the client functionality as the 004 and 005 events arrive?

> Adding tracking of joined channels and users in those channels

I can think of some applications that wouldn't require this functionality. If it's easy enough to abstract away this behavior for clients that don't need it, then perhaps it makes sense. My instinct is if there's a clear abstraction between low level functionality (handling of events) and higher-level functionality (tracking channels, users) those classes of functionality should be implemented in separate Python Classes. I can see value in the default, recommended "Client" class having this higher level functionality.

> Moving of bot.py into irc/contrib/ and removal of server.py

The bot seems to be the single most used class in the library. Do you figure the only reason that bot is use is for the user/channel management?

Removing server is a frustrating change. The irc library itself uses the server to run some tests. Other libraries also use the server for the purpose of running tests. By adopting the server, the irc library set out to be more than a simple client library, but rather to supply the best-in-platform library for behavior relating to the protocol. If the server is removed, then another server needs to supplant it. I'm disinclined to create a separate maintenance track for the server when this library hosts it just fine with minimal complication.

> Removal of scheduled function runs

This is an intriguing idea. I was initially opposed because I know this functionality is used heavily in some bot implementations. But if the IRC library could provide a forward-compatible implementation that relies on a recommended third-party scheduler, that would be excellent, removing redundancy.


Overall, I'd like to tackle each of these changes independently, rather than one huge backward-incompatible release. I'm sure you won't be excited about contributing some of these changes to a library that you already consider inferior to your own (and for good reasons), but incremental changes toward a known target should allow consuming applications to adapt with the changes incrementally and allow issues to be raised and resolved along the way. Would that be a process in which you'd be willing to work?

Hi, thanks for the question. This happens because gdb is run as a subprocess, which causes event handling for new data to stdout/stderr to become less reliable.

There are a few options, you can can experiment with them and see which help
* interrupt gdb with the [`interrupt_gdb` method](https://grassfedcode.com/pygdbmi/api/pygdbmi.html#pygdbmi.gdbcontroller.GdbController.interrupt_gdb) of the GdbController class
* force flush output check for output with `-data-evaluate-expression fflush(0)` or `call fflush(0)`. I think this needs to be run after interrupting.
* periodically call the [`get_gdb_response` method](https://grassfedcode.com/pygdbmi/api/pygdbmi.html#pygdbmi.gdbcontroller.GdbController.get_gdb_response) of the GdbController class, with `raise_error_on_timeout=False`. Similar to the previous bullet, you may have to interrupt gdb before running this.

Let me know if you get it to work.

I don't want to give the impression that I'm not concerned about reliability, but I'm not sure it's the role of the Curio kernel to solve every possible problem that might occur on the network.   In the big picture, having multiple tasks trying to read or multiple tasks trying to write on the same socket at the same time is already a major application-level bug in my opinion. It makes no sense for stream-based I/O unless you want everything randomly garbled.   At the moment, Curio raises an exception if you try to do this.  It's maybe not ideal, but it's a step above asyncio where a second attempt to perform an operation (i.e., two reads on the same socket) seems to result in the first operation being silently cancelled.

With respect to SSL, it's not clear to me that it supports full-duplex I/O in any kind of traditional sense.  I found this thread: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/mailing.openssl.users/scU_UV-VuGc

It's pretty dense, but the gist of it that I get is that an SSL connection is running an underlying state machine.  Within this state machine, it's not possible for multiple threads to be doing different things on an SSL connection at the same time (i.e., one thread reading, one thread writing).  It's a little less clear with respect to async/non-blocking, but similar rules may be in effect.  For example, if you start an SSL write and it comes back with a WantRead condition, you have to honor that and continue to proceed with the steps involved in the write operation until it completes successfully.  Only after it's fully done, can you then proceed to a new operation such as a read/write.     One way to manage this in the presence of threads/tasks might be to protect read/writes with a mutex lock. Or to use some kind of queuing.   

It's entirely possible that my interpretation of this is wrong, but either way, it all sounds rather hairy to me.  I think my inclination is simply to have Curio fail loudly with an exception if there's some kind of resource conflict (i.e., two tasks attempting to read on the same socket).  Leave it up to the user to figure out how to negotiate the conflict.

I think, I'll go into the to very basics again.
This is my perspective, however I hope it can
help where to draw the line.

- As I previously mentioned, blocking sync code
is the king. However it has one drawback: It runs on
threads.

- For me async programming is 'only' about eliminating
threads. Luckly, with Curio only adding "awaits" to
blocking operations does it all. (Not so simple. However
you get the idea.)

- At this point I naturally expect all async calls to behave
EXACTLY as the blocking counterparts.

- Any slightest EAGER/CLEVER/GREEDY BEHAVIOUR
will directly bring A CONTROL/COORDINATION ISSUE,
which is I think we all are trying to describe here.

- Although I did not had an issue (perhaps because of
my style or the program structure) if any smallest
amount of cleverness/eagerness is going under the
covers I'll soon hit it.

- Not similar but same class problem with asyncio
is sync write(). It buffers the bytes and you should be
doing some other await ing or sleep(0) to flush it.
Even worse it has min-max buffer sizes to tell where
to start and stop... It is sure nasty. It had diverged
from the elagance of pure sync write() drastically.

- So for Curio the moral of the story:

await revc/read()
await recv/read(1000)
await send/write()

should not do anything more than blocking
counterparts. And should return only after
really completing the operation.

And the kernel should only do mere enough to
satisfity these calls. There shouldn't be any,
"if there is some data lets get it" attitude.

- Some of you may not like the idea. However,
providing a coordination pattern is far more
nasty.

- Curio should never break "One-to-one direct link"
between user code and data traffic. User code is the
only responsible for the traffic. Never interfere with
it even in slightest terms.

As far as Stride specifically, there *is* no spec, so it's impossible to say.

I've contacted their team directly and submitted a [support topic](https://community.atlassian.com/t5/Stride-questions/Stride-XMPP-and-API-support-and-timeline/qaq-p/657424).  

I've heard back in private email (but not the public topic), and here's what I know thus far (it's mostly marketing speak):


> There are no planned changes to the HipChat XMPP protocol. If you are seeing inconsistencies, they are probably not intentional. If you file a support request with details, we can try to help you track it down. Feel free to mention this ticket for context.
> 
> Unlike HipChat, Stride is not built using XMPP and will not support an XMPP interface. Instead, 
>If you'd like early access to that API, please sign up here and we'll get you in:     https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/stride/
> 
> HipChat users will be moving to Stride group-by-group, on their own timetable. We've just become the process of inviting folks to migrate. In building Stride, however, we realized that we needed to make some fundamental changes in order to provide the kind of capabilities and dev experience that we wanted. We understand that it will mean work for developers to support Stride, but we believe the long term enhancements will be worth it.
> 

I'll keep this issue updated as we know more - if anyone is game to take on owning the Stride backend, or has more information, please chime in!

Structure IDentifier (SID) are used to map YANG definitions to
CBOR encoding. These SIDs can be automatically generated
for a YANG module using the pyang sid plugin.

   pyang --generate-sid-file <entry-point:size> <yang-module>

For example:
   pyang --generate-sid-file 20000:100 toaster@2009-11-20.yang

The name of the .sid file generated is:

   <module-name>@<module-revision>.sid

Each time new items(s) are added to a YANG module by the
introduction of a new revision of this module, its included
sub-module(s) or imported module(s), the .sid file need to be
updated. This is done by providing the name of the previous
.sid file as argument.

   pyang --update-sid-file <file-name> <yang-module>

For example:
   pyang --update-sid-file toaster@2009-11-20.sid toaster@2009-12-28.yang

The --check-sid-file option can be used at any time to verify
if the .sid file need to be updated.

   pyang --check-sid-file <file-name> <yang-module>

The --list_sid option can be included before any of the previous
option to obtains the list of SIDs assigned or validated.

For example:
   pyang --list-sid --generate-sid-file 20000:100 toaster@2009-11-20.yang

Regarding 1, yes that is correct. copyto will copy regardless. This is because some files must be assumed to exist in the working directory. I agree with the criticism that copyto doesn't protect from several cores writing to the same location. I thought a lot about that and different schemes to get around it, but they all become increasingly complicated with little benefit. In practice I find it unlikely that two cores will use copyto with the same destination file. If that happens, it will likely be detected and if both core authors refuse to change their name, you could overload one of the cores with a modified .core file that uses another name in worst case. To mitigate this further I should probably add some best practices documentation that tells users to voluntarily namespace their files with a sanitized VLNV or similar.

It should be technically possible to do 2. I haven't thought this through, but it does feel a bit strange to use the file type as a filter (file type is definitely better than matching suffices or trying to capture files under a hdl alias.). My preferred solution here is still to use the copyto attribute, but we can still elaborate on the second idea to see if file type is the best filter. What you _really_ want to do in this particular case is more like _only_ export the xci files, and that's basically what copyto does

Following further research, ``librosa`` does not handle the problem. It does include a scale parameter but that parameter does not guarantee a min or max amplitude.

The solution I found, supported by the popular library SoX, is either selectively lowering the volume (gain) when resampling causes a large min/max or to clip the amplitude at [-1, 1].

I think this is probably expected behavior given how short your input signals are, but I will look into it.  (Your code example does reproduce the reported behavior on my environment.)

To get high quality reconstruction, the default filter (`kaiser_best`) uses a very long window to approximate a sinc filter.  When you get to the end of the signal, the filter gets adaptively shortened to avoid running off the end of the input buffer, which results in the attenuation behavior that you reported above.  You can see this by switching from `kaiser_best` to `kaiser_fast`, in which case the results look like:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1190540/40922949-96744acc-67e1-11e8-921c-39df1449743a.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1190540/40922950-98541e62-67e1-11e8-95f3-23e20fd082ca.png)
(note that the edge effects occupy a much smaller portion of the signal).  If you want even more control over this, you can call `resampy(... filter='sinc_window', num_zeros=16)`; the `num_zeros` parameter controls the effective precision of the resampling filter, with larger values producing longer filters and better fidelity.

For typical audio signals, these edge effects should be negligible.  ~100 samples at 22050 Hz would not be audible.  Still, it's a bit strange that the beginning of the resampled signal does not exhibit these artifacts, since the filter is time-symmetric, so I'll look into it.

Hi promethianfire. I don't believe there's any way to move the dependency. keyring requires pytest-runner _before_ tests_requires is even invoked, so for the test dependencies to declare a dependency on pytest runner would not help.

The point of a 'setup_requires' dependency is to signal to setuptools that this requirement is necessary when setup is run (for build/test/install commands or to provide additional commands). The only package that could possibly require or bundle pytest-runner is setuptools itself, but that's not going to happen (because that approach isn't scalable).

But perhaps there's something that can be done to ease the burden on Gentoo. Since the package is only required when running setup, it should not be required at run time (similar to tests_require) nor if keyring is installed by a system package manager (such as a Windows installer or Linux system package). This is generally true for all 'setup_requires' dependencies.

This fact gives Gentoo the opportunity to run setup.py once to generate the package (pulling in pytest-runner during that step) but then bundle the build and disregard pytest-runner thereafter.

Can you clarify when the dependency on pytest runner is problemmatic? Is it during the preparation of Gentoo packages or later when the users install keyring?

I'm still exploring this landscape myself and trying to understand the complications imposed by 'setup_requires' declarations. I use the declaration in many packages throughout my work every day, and it works well, but I work in an environment which has access to the packages it needs and installs them with pip/setuptools directly. I expect I might even want to add to the setup_requires dependencies. If doing so is going to complicate the system package maintainers, I'd like to know what those complications are or devise a way to minimize that effect.

Can you elaborate on the challenges this dependency creates?

This error most likely means that you have gnome-keyring installed and running, but it does not have any actual keyring created.

Create one and the error would be gone (though I agree that keyring should handle such errors properly).

Try something like this


```
#!python

def securityCmd(command, *args):
    p1 = Popen(
        ["/usr/bin/security", "-i"], stdout=PIPE, stderr=STDOUT, stdin=PIPE)
    cmd = command + " " + " ".join(list(args)) + "\n"
    output = p1.communicate(cmd)[0]
    return p1.returncode, output
```


This runs security in interactive mode and sends the command to security's stdin in the communicate() call. Does not expose any command line arguments (other than "-i").

One difference I found was that by default matlab uses the sample standard deviation and numpy uses the population standard deviation if you don't include extra arguments, which caused the results of some functions to differ slightly.

Another difference is that the libraries used to do the eigendecomposition in matlab and numpy return similar but nonidentical results, which caused e.g. the modular decomposition in certain functions to differ slightly between the two implementations.

Is this the kind of functionality you have in mind?

```python
>>> import pydash
>>> def sort_by_many(collection, iteratees=None, reverse=False):
...     if not iteratees:
...         key = None
...     else:
...         def key(obj):
...             def reducer(result, iteratee):
...                 return result + (pydash.iteratee(iteratee)(obj),)
...             return pydash.reduce_(iteratees, reducer, ())
...     
...     return sorted(collection, key=key, reverse=reverse)
... 
>>> items = [{'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'a': 1, 'b': 1}, {'a': 2, 'b': 0}]
>>> sort_by_many(items, ['a', 'b'])
[{'a': 1, 'b': 1}, {'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'a': 2, 'b': 0}]
>>> sort_by_many(items, ['b', 'a'])
[{'a': 2, 'b': 0}, {'a': 1, 'b': 1}, {'a': 1, 'b': 2}]
```

Essentially, it reduces the `iteratees` into a tuple of values that are used as the sort key for the `collection`.

Because the data is stored in a dictionary the data is inherently unordered. Because of the structure of the returned data you can easily sort the data. Either in place using the `your_list.sort()` function of make a new list from it by wrapping in in `sorted(your_list)`.

```python
global_thread = QThread.currentThread()
called = False
class MyObject(QObject):
    def mycallback(self):
        global global_thread, mythread, called
        cur_thread = QThread.currentThread()
        assert cur_thread is not global_thread
        assert cur_thread is mythread
        called = True

class Signalizer(QObject):
    signaling = pyqtSignal()

mythread = QThread()
mythread.start()
myobject = MyObject()
myobject.moveToThread(mythread)
signer = Signalizer()
signer.signaling.connect(myobject.mycallback)

@asyncio.coroutine
def mycoroutine():
    signer.signaling.emit()

loop = quamash.QEventLoop(app)
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
with loop:
    loop.run_until_complete(mycoroutine())
assert called is True
```

Hello @Engi4, if your goal is only to merge different files that may have similar cell names, the simplest solution is to use the `rename` argument in [gds_import](http://gdspy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/io.html#gdsimport).

Something around these lines (I modified the example you presented in #16):

```python
import gdspy

operations = {
    'Dcsfq.gds': {'origin': (150, 0)},
    'Conf2.gds': {'origin': (0, 0)},
    'Tff4b.gds': {'origin': (225, -150), 'rotation': 90}
}

ref_cell = gdspy.Cell('TRANSLATED')

for i, file_name in enumerate(operations):
    # import file to get cell names
    gdsii = gdspy.GdsImport(file_name)
    # append all cell names with the 'i' counter
    names = {c: '{}_{}'.format(c, i) for c in gdsii.cell_dict}
    # just out of curiosity:
    print('\nRenaming cells in ' + file_name + ':')
    print(names)

    # import again renaming all cells and extract the result
    gdsii = gdspy.GdsImport(file_name, rename=names)
    cell = gdsii.extract(gdsii.top_level()[0])

    # apply the respective operation
    ref_cell.add(gdspy.CellReference(cell, **operations[file_name]))

gdspy.gds_print('new_gds.gds', cells=[ref_cell], unit=1.0e-6, precision=1.0e-9)
```

I ended up making a Tokenizer that looks like this, and then using `.tokens` instead of `.words`:

```
import re

from nltk.tokenize.treebank import TreebankWordTokenizer
from textblob.utils import strip_punc


class ContractionPreservingTokenizer(TreebankWordTokenizer):
    ENDING_QUOTES = [
        (re.compile(r'"'), " '' "),
        (re.compile(r'(\S)(\'\')'), r'\1 \2 '),
    ]

    def tokenize(self, text):
        tokens = super().tokenize(text)
        return [word if word.startswith("'") else strip_punc(word, all=False)
                for word in tokens if strip_punc(word, all=False)]
```

It seems this is probably the best solution with the current codebase.

Jotting implementation thoughts for how this can work:

1. Compute magnitude spectrograms for all sources.  We can be sloppy here, and use scipy.signal.spectrogram

Yes, that is something I've been thinking about for a long time. One project I'm aware of which provides a "pure" C layer with on top the bindings for other languages is [sigar](https://github.com/hyperic/sigar/tree/master/bindings).
psutil is getting to a point which is pretty much feature-complete. The next more logical step in terms of new functionality would be adding support for some exotic platform (e.g. [cygwin](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/998)) or start porting it to other languages (which [is already happening](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil#portings) BTW).
That's not a simple task though:
- cPython API is used extensively throughout all C code
- the C code should return basic C types instead
- the implementation for platforms such as [Linux](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/_pslinux.py) is 80% Python. That would have to be rewritten from scratch in C.
- Other platforms such as [Windows](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/_pswindows.py) also have a non-negligible amount of Python logic which would have to be rewritten in C.

> How much effort would you estimate for this task? 

A lot. I imagine this as a 3-step process:
1) implement the pure C layer meaning getting rid of the cPython API and moving the Python logic to C. This may be a separate `psutil-lib` project or it may grow inside psutil itself.
2) add a Python wrapper on top of it
3) gradually add wrappers for other languages
I'm not sure how long this would take but it's surely in the order of *months* assuming a full-time investment.

Hello, sorry for the late response to this issue.

The scripts `/usr/local/bin/keymaker-create-account-for-iam-user` and `/usr/sbin/keymaker-get-public-keys` are not meant to be called directly.

The first script is called by the PAM authentication stack when trying to log in the user. The currently released version of keymaker incorrectly raises errors in `keymaker-create-account-for-iam-user` even when the process succeeds. It's safe to disregard these errors. You can check whether the process succeeded or not by trying to log in with a user with a name corresponding to an IAM user name, but no local user account. If the account is created after trying to log in, then the script is working fine.

Your second issue means that /usr/sbin/keymaker-get-public-keys failed to retrieve the keys for the user. This script is called by sshd when trying to log in the user. The failure to retrieve keys can happen because you did not upload the user's public key with `keymaker upload`, the IAM role for your instance doesn't have read access to the IAM user object, or the instance has no network access to the IAM API. (I do need to figure out a better way for these processes to log their errors).

Before I respond to your specific feature requests, let me explain a few things. Hashes in Redis are very limited in what they can do, and don't play nicely with sets or zsets. Things like `query.filter()`, `.like()`, `.startswith()`, `.endswith()`, `.order_by()`, and `.limit()` uses sets or zsets in a variety of ways to turn the result into a zset - which is trivially paginated over with `ZRANGE`. Hashes don't have arbitrary "give me 30 items starting from offset n/2" (though like you say, `ZSCAN` does let you incrementally scan a hash, and it is conditionally used in at least one place in rom already), so a typical `.limit()` clause won't work. The only case that makes sense is if someone were to do something like `Model.query.order_by('unique_index').iter_result()`

* Re: Using unique indexes for `query.count()`

`None` values are not stored in the unique index, and do not count as unique values. So any entity that has `None` as a value in a unique indexed column wouldn't be counted by a `query.count()`. Requiring `required=True` might be okay, but there is something else that can be used.

A little bit deeper, there is a separate hash that actually has a mapping from all entity ids to the json encoded version of raw index data (used for un-indexing to ensure that all known index data is cleared). That said, some fairly old versions of rom didn't clean this index data properly, and other users like to delete the underlying hash key that stores the rom entity itself, so in some cases *that* result could be incorrect too.

To offer `Model.query.count()` functionality, it may be sufficient to document that users upgrading from very old versions of rom or those deleting keys directly (instead of using `entity.delete()`) will need to run `all(rom.util.clean_old_index(model))` after upgrading/deleting in order to get a correct answer. I can add this.

* Re: HSCAN

Using HSCAN only really makes sense if someone uses `Model.query.iter_result()` to iterate over the results, as allowing `Model.query.all()` is a bad idea.

* Re: SCAN

SCAN is unnecessary, see the hash of id -> index data mentioned earlier.

I'm unsure what you mean by "and index records". If you mean refreshing indexes, rom already has a utility function for re-indexing data if you've changed your index fields, it's called `util.refresh_indices(model)`, and can be used like `all(util.refresh_indices(model))` .This doesn't use HSCAN simply because there is a very good chance that for any nontrivial rom use, the values in *any* index can change during iteration, so we can't rely on them. Right now it actually uses `Model.get([<a range of ids that may or may not exist>])`, which could be improved.

* Re: `Model.count()` and `for i in Model`

What if it was spelled `Model.query.count()` and `for i in Model.query.iter_results()` or even `for i in Model.query`? I ask because right now all of the "get me information about this group of things" interface is hanging off the `Model.query` attribute, and I can foresee someone already utilizing the `.count` attribute on a model for existing data. No such limitation or concern with modifying `Model.query`.

A couple things here.  First, the internal units for all angles in TreeCorr is radians, so accessing `cat_data.ra[i]` and `cat_data_dec[i]` will show the value in radians, not degrees.  That explains the factor of 57 you saw.

The bigger issue you are seeing is that the reported separations for the final correlation function are technically the chord distances across the unit sphere rather than the great circle angle.  cf. the [documentation of the `Euclidean` metric](http://rmjarvis.github.io/TreeCorr/html/nn.html#treecorr.NNCorrelation.process), although I admit the documentation about this fact is probably too light currently.

For small angles, they are basically equivalent, so it doesn't make much difference.  But with angles of 90 or 180 degrees, it makes a bit difference.  An angle of 180 degrees is pi radians.  But the chord distance is only 2 (the diameter).  If you ask for this in degrees, it will multiply by 180/pi to get 115, not 180.  With the binning, I think this is why it looked like a maximum of 128 degrees.  Probably it was really a maximum of 115 "degrees", which means a maximum of 2 for the chord distances.

The reason for this is that it is much faster to compute distances in Euclidean coordinates rather than in spherical coordinates, so we put all the points on the unit sphere and then use Euclidean separations for everything.  I should probably change this so that it at least converts back to the great circle distance at the end, but I haven't yet, since most people are using separations < 10 degrees where it doesn't make a big difference.

In the meanwhile, you can convert the reported distances yourself using the formula:

    r_GC = 2 * math.asin(0.5 * r_chord)

where both `r_GC` and `r_chord` are in radians.

Once the game goes final, `nflgame` flips the switch on that game and makes no attempt to ever see if the game has been updated in anyway.

One thing you can **try** (heavy emphasis intended) is to delete that game from the `gamecenter-json` and see if the game downloads again with the complete set of plays.  But your mileage may very on this since I don't believe the game center json feed is actually the source of play-by-play data; it only feeds the game center.  In most cases, especially stat corrections, the NFL will modify the play-by-play found on pages like the one you linked without modifying the game-center json that we rely on.

```python
import nflgame

games = nflgame.games(2016, 1, 'SEA', 'SEA', 'REG')
plays = nflgame.combine_plays(games)

print [game.eid for game in games]
print 'Final 5 plays:'
for play in list(plays)[-5:]:
    print play
```

TestSimpleOffsets was the first suite where I encountered tests that set flags on `Constants`. I added the ability to specify those in YAML as we discussed earlier. This just uses `setattr` to set any attribute to whatever value is specified.

In this example, the second test group uses a different option.

```yaml
offset_from_day_of_week:
    sourceTime: *tuesdays
    cases:
        - target: !datedelta
            days: 2
          context: !pdtContext day
          phrases:
            - "Thursday"
        - target: !datedelta
            # TODO: Handling this as Thursday at 9 plus 1 hour seems
            # unexpected.
            sourceTime: !replace xxxx-xx-xx 09:00:00
            days: 2
            hours: 1
          context: !pdtContext day | hour
          phrases:
            - "one hour from Thursday"
        - target: !datedelta
            # TODO: Handling this as Thursday at 9 minus 1 hour seems
            # unexpected.
            sourceTime: !replace xxxx-xx-xx 09:00:00
            days: 2
            hours: -1
          context: !pdtContext day | hour
          phrases:
            - "one hour before Thursday"

offset_from_day_of_week_matching_source_time:
    sourceTime: *tuesdays
    options:
        StartTimeFromSourceTime: True
    cases:
        - target: !datedelta
            # TODO: Handling this as Thursday at the current time plus 1 hour
            # seems unexpected.
            days: 2
            hours: 1
          context: !pdtContext day | hour
          phrases:
            - "one hour from Thursday"
        - target: !datedelta
            # TODO: Handling this is Thursday at the current time minus 1 hour
            # seems unexpected.
            days: 2
            hours: -1
          context: !pdtContext day | hour
          phrases:
            - "one hour before Thursday"
```

Dang - that'll require a fix on my side.  I very carefully rewrote Unicode handling for *data*, but it looks like I missed the meta data.  I'll try to work on it today.

This is actually not an error - floating point values often can't store a value that terminates in base 10 that don't terminate in base 2.  The most famous example is probably 0.1, which is actually not possible to store in a floating point number exactly - it repeats in base 2.  Therefore many exact values in base 10, apparently 1.61 is an example, must be approximated in a float.

The difference you are seeing is simply rounding for display.  Both systems are retrieving 1.6099999 but the DB is displaying it using something like `print('%0.2f', val)` which rounds to 2 digits after the decimal.

I'm not sure what database you are using, but it might have some decent documentation in its floating point data types section.  If you need exact values, such as when dealing with money, consider using the "numeric" or "decimal" data type in your database and the Python Decimal class.

After doing some more tests on our ends, it seems the issue is occurring when connecting to Microsoft SQL Server databases. We connect dsn-less with the following connection string:

`Driver={FreeTDS};Server=xxx;Port=1433;TDS_Version=7.0;UID=xxx;PWD=xxx;`

There are three issues:
* pyodbc doesn't have array bindings yet
* I don't think ODBC / SQL Server will actually process an array in an IN statement which is why I didn't bother with the array bindings.
* pyodbc is unpacking the list since that is how the DBAPI requires parameters.

I'll retest IN clauses with array parameters, but I'm pretty sure SQL Server wouldn't take it.  If it does, I'd like to have it myself so I'll add it immediately.  If not I'll update this.

I end up constructing the IN clause like so:

    params = [...]
    qmarks = ','.join('?' * len(params))
    sql = "select ... in {}".format(qmarks)

I'm not a fan, but I was pretty sure it was required.  I'll check again because *I'd* really like to not have to do that also.

The last point is that the DBAPI requires execute only have 2 parameters, (sql, params), where params is a sequence of parameters.  When pyodbc sees a single parameter that is a sequence, for compatibility with the API it will unpack it.  For those cases wrap it in another sequence:

  execute("...", ( [1,2,3,4] ))

If you are working in a section of code that has a variable number of parameters and you are generating SQL, etc. and you don't want to special case this kind of code, simply *always* use an extra sequence.  This works fine:

    execute("select * from u where id=?", (1))

Obviously when writing by hand, which is most of the time, I prefer not to use the outer sequence.
In any case, to summarise, this is not an issue in pyODBC, but rather by-design behaviour of Python language itself.

Draft EEP ;-)

EEP: 1
Title: Standard for the use of logging in Eppy 
Version: 0.0.1
Last-Modified: 17 Jun 2015
Author: Jamie Bull <jamie.bull@oco-carbon.com>
Status: Draft
Type: Standards Track
Created: 17-Jun-2015
Eppy-Version: >=0.4.6.4a

Abstract
    This inaugural Eppy Enhancement Proposal (EEP) puts forward use cases
    for logging, from both a developer and a user perspective.
    
    The proposal is not that logging is made a requirement, just that logging
    can be implemented easily and consistently where a developer sees a need.
    
Rationale
    Logging has two main use cases, each of which has unique characteristics
    that mean it should be treated differently.
    
    In the case of developers, when errors arise, the stack trace often does not
    contain enough information to understand what has caused the problem. In
    these situations, logging can reveal not only the call stack, but also
    information about the data and parameters. Unexpected values could suggest
    the need for additional tests and/or input validation.
    
    This could be followed in real time by viewing the tail of a log file or by
    viewing the output to stdout.
    
    For users, a track of what is happening in their program can be useful when
    learning how Eppy software does what it does. They may also appreciate being
    able to submit a log along with a bug report, and this should allow
    contributors to understand the source of errors more quickly.
    
    A user may also want to refer to more detailed logs to at a later time,
    particularly useful when using Eppy to generate large numbers of IDFs from
    variable and perhaps invalid inputs.
    
Proposed standards
    1. Logging module
       All logging should use a logging configuration file. This could either be
       directly in a python file (quicker to code), or alternatively an .ini
       file (easier for users to edit). This will help to ensure a consistent
       logging format is followed across modules. To implement logging within a
       module, all that needs to be added is the following code:

           import eppy.log_config  # module location tbd
	   
	   # create a module-level logger 
           logger = log_config.get_logger(__name__)
	   
	   # used as follows
           logger.debug("This is a DEBUG level message")
           logger.info("This is an INFO level message")
       
    2. Log levels
       The level for a logging call should generally follow those recommended by
       the logging module documentation, "Logging HOWTO" [1] in the Python
       documentation. For the most part, developers will be interested in the
       DEBUG level messages which contain details of data and parameters,
       while users will likely be more concerned with INFO level messages which
       track the 10000 ft view of activity.
       
    3. Verbosity
       Given that there are at least two use cases, and that logs can grow
       very large, there should be a verbosity flag that can be set to hide
       messages below a given level. Messages could be hidden either in a log
       file for a specific logging level, or by not logging them at all (e.g.
       not logging at the debug level for general users). This should be set in
       the config file and should be configurable during runtime.
       
References
    [1] Logging HOWTO, Vinay Sajip, https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html

Copyright
    This document has been placed in the public domain.

Yes. The key thing here is what slide masters and slide layouts you have available to you at run time.

When you add the slides having a different theme, their slide master and slide layouts are automatically added to the file too. You just don't see them unless you go looking for them in the Slide Master view. Once that's done, you get rid of the slides you added so you can start with an empty presentation. Deleting the slides does *not* remove the new masters and layouts. Once they're in they're there to stay unless you delete them manually. All this is done "by hand" using PowerPoint, just to get your template file configured correctly.

There are other ways to do it--any method that gets the masters and layouts in there is fine--but this way is probably the quickest.

What you're really looking for is how the starting presentation looks in Slide Master view. In regular Slide view mode, it should be empty when you're done. Slide Master view is also where you can figure out what the index of a particular master or layout is; just remember to start counting at 0 instead of 1.

Note there is at least one other part involved, the theme{n}.xml files. Those specify the palette and fonts used by the master and layouts for that theme, and perhaps a few other details. Those get added automatically by this process. I just mention them here for anyone who might take a more direct approach :)

On Wed, Jun 1, 2016 at 6:54 AM, Colin Raffel <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> Thanks for pointing this out. I think the issue is that this snippet you
> removed and the new stable sorting code are doing the opposite thing - the
> stable sort always puts a note on event after a note off event when they
> have the same tick, and this snippet always puts the note off after the
> note on. The initial justification for putting note offs second is to avoid
> a stuck note (e.g. a note-on with no corresponding note-off). I think the
> justification behind putting note-ons second is to prevent the artificial
> removal of a note. Does that make sense/are you with me?
>
> I think the actual correct thing to do would be to determine the ordering
> based on whether a note-on appears before or not. For example, in this
> scenario:
>
> O ------- O/F ------- F ...
>
> where "O" is a note on and "F" is a note off and "O/F" is a note on and
> note off with the same tick (all notes having the same pitch of course), we
> should put the "F" first, because then we have two complete notes. In this
> scenario:
>
> O ----- F ------ O/F ------- O ------ F ---- ...
>
> we should really just remove both the note on and the note off event with
> the same tick, because they correspond to a note which has zero duration.

No, there is no 1:1 analogy between an object in pretty_midi and a MIDI track.  MIDI tracks are a pretty useless concept (conceptually) because they can contain an arbitrary number of different instruments over the course of the track.  Each instrument (according to program changes) on each track on each channel gets turned into a separate Instrument by pretty_midi.  If you want low-level access to MIDI primitives, I'd suggest using mido directly.

Hi,

I just went over the code and I understand where everything comes from.

Logic:
  - Move the dataset(s) 1 step forward
  - Execute any orders
  - If something got executed, notify it to the strategy
  - run the strategy's `next` method

Following this logic you should get your result. But I looked deeper and realized that the `Close` order has an execution logic which takes into account the "end of session" time. This is so to make sure that it supports intraday data feeds, in which several bars for the same day will be delivered and the order has to execute when the last bar of the day has been seen.

The default "end of session" time is 23:59:59 and therefore the broker (which is only a simulation) has to wait for the next days (because you are using "daily bars") to decide if the order was executed at `Close`.

Possible solutions

  - You put a "time" in your daily bars and declare an "end of session" before the time present in the daily bars

or/and

  - extend the execution logic for "Close" bars to look at the declared "timeframe" of the bars. In the case of daily bars the order will be executed during the bar currently being executed and your strategy will be promptly notified.

Declaring the "end of session" time for a data feed:

```python
data = MyDataFeed(dataname='mydatafile', sessionend=datetime.time(15, 0, 0))
```

> I don't understand when you say :
>> When there is server status update after the 'DONE' is sent it is appended to the response to the 'IDLE'

> It shouldn't because when DONE is sent, you are exiting the IDLE command so the server should not send status update.

The server does not send status updates to the client directly, but it'll attach these status updates to the next answer he sends out.
The stackoverflow answer which started my debugging:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2513194/imap-idle-timeout?answertab=votes#tab-top

And the rfc3501 for that:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3501#section-5.2

In the particular case I'm talking about, the next message after the 'DONE' ist the restart of the IDLE loop and the 'IDLE' message. So all status updates are added to the response of this IDLE message. But this message is consumed somewhere in the aioimaplib (or I did not found the function/return value where this data can be retrieved) the `idle.result()` does not include those messages. This response is e.g. 
'GMDM59 OK Idle completed (10.012 + 10.012 + 10.011 secs).' Not the answer to the starting 'IDLE' command send to the server.

Recently I have been playing with Home Assistant. I added some custom components and modified some. One of the ones I modified was the plex component. I added the ability to direct a plex client to play a configured item (e.g. workout video). This tied with Alexa allows you to say - "Alexa, turn on workout video" - which in our house setup can invoke a script to turn on lights, whole home speakers, a TV, tune the Roku to the Plex app, and launch the workout video. This all works. And this plex component (that I did not write btw) is heavily reliant on plexapi.

I posted this update write-up on the Home Assistant forum and someone asked about being able to launch plex with a playlist. I wrote up how to modify the code to do that. Then they asked for shuffle support - one of the enhancements I requested here.

There is a new plexdevice component someone is working on to make the Home Assistant plex view look nicer. I decided it would be nice to add the live update and started playing with that. I had something working but knew it would be best if there was better support in plexapi.

I would like these Home Assistant components to use the plexapi as much as possible without having to parse xml directly or interface with requests separately. There might be tokens or headers that are missed or messed up. It just seems cleaner to go through a consistent interface.

Oh I see! That has to do with the fact that using `ainput` within an asynchronous console will remove the `stdin` file descriptor from the event loop at the end. The console won't be notified of any user input after that. Only `Ctrl+C` will work, since it's goes through the signaling mechanism. For some reasons, this is not the case for `Ctrl+D` (see [this part of the code](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aioconsole/blob/master/aioconsole/code.py#L191-L192)).

Do you see an actual use case for using `ainput` within an asynchronous console?

> I am confused about what the value-add is over ffmpeg.

If you want to do simple peak or RMS normalization, you first have to analyze the file, parse the ffmpeg log output, calculate the required offset to your target, then normalize the file with the `volume` filter. Easy enough to do one, but if you want to do that on many files, you have to write a somewhat complex script. This is basically what `ffmpeg-normalize` does – a wrapper around ffmpeg to call it twice and do some maths, on many files.

The addition of mappings to use the `loudnorm` filter (for EBU R128 normalization) was just added for convenience. Same as the option to specify extra arguments to ffmpeg, which is what you are doing with `dynaudnorm`. Under the hood it's using ffmpeg, so there's no benefit from using `ffmpeg-normalize`, really – at least in terms of audio quality or extra options that you'd get.

>  if you happen to know of similar scripts to remove background noise or other artifacts of amateur recordings, I would be glad to know about them

In these cases I usually play with what's built into Premiere Pro or Cubase, as I happen to use these programs for video and audio editing. I don't know of a denoise filter for ffmpeg; a low-pass filter may help for certain types of wind noise, and an EQ with a very high Q setting can help reducing hiss or some annoying frequency, but usually you want something more adaptive.

TLDR;
- type conversion - nope.
- striping - go for it.

---

__Automatic Type Conversion__

While this is useful in most cases, I am afraid this feature is under the scope of this project. The goal of this project is:

> Read the data from `.env` and export it as as environment variable. Once they are injected as environment variables, they can be read by any python code any where in your codebase, using `os.environ.get()`, etc. 

Things put in operating system's environment variable are always `string`.

There is an nice interface provided by https://github.com/joke2k/django-environ to read the available environment variables and then cast them to desired datatype. I would encourage you to have a look at it. 

__Striping whitespaces__

We can have it enabled via an optional parameter (`strip_whitespaces=True`) to `load_dotenv` function with default value as `False`. Happy to see a pull request. :blush: :+1:

Hey @byt3bl33d3r,

I performed some tests and it seems that:
* Your script is easily portable to Windows
* It compiles and work flawlessly, at least on W7 and W10 though I haven't tried advanced intrusion techniques

First, in order to port your script, you need to:
1. Remove (or adapt) the line 2998 as ` os.geteuid()` does not exist on Windows
2. Patch the code section related to term colors 
from 
```
from termcolor import cprint, colored
```
to
```
from termcolor import cprint, colored
import colorama
colorama.init()
```

* Install:
  * [python-2.7.10.msi](https://www.python.org/ftp/python/2.7.10/python-2.7.10.msi)

Revisting this, I now have a hard time seeing how the `register()` on a folder could work. As far as I understand, an extended property is something you register globally on the server, but the value is something that is attached to individual items. Thus, extended properties is not something that you can automatically attach to items in a folder. A folder may contain a mix of items, some of which have a certain extended property, ans some that don't. If you move an item from one folder to another, the new folder knows nothing about extended properties on the moved item.

I think the only sane approach here is to place the `register()` on the `Item` class. After calling `register()`, all new items would get the custom attribute, but right now I can't see any severe downsides to that. Calling `deregister()` would possibly remove access to that attribute on existing item instances, though.

Hi @amesbrown ,

this questions was raised many times and yes, Pillow uses 75% JPEG compression by default. That isn't something StdImage specific.
The reason why there isn't a `quality` parameter is simple, only JPEG has one and StdImage supports all files supported by Pillow (PNG, BPM, JPEG2000, ...).

If you want to change the default, you can pass your own `render_variations` callable, which might change the behavior including the JPEG quality.

Besides that, 75% is a pretty good default.

Hello,

On permission system please take a look at:
http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid/en/1.5-branch/narr/security.html#protecting-views-with-permissions

and my tutorial:
http://pyramid-blogr.readthedocs.org/en/latest/authorization.html

It works a bit different in pyramid in general and it can be a concept that is a bit weird for someone just starting with pyramid.

Yes, you use a decorator on your view to specify permission like:
```python
   @view_config(route_name='admin', match_param='action=view_user',
             renderer='json', permission='some_perm')
    def admin_user_view(request):
```

at this point pyramid will know that for this request the CONTEXT object need to supply __acl__ property containing list of entities that have permissions to your page/resource - and then it will be matched with your user object.

So when dealing with URL ROUTING in pyramid you need to specify a context factory:

```python
    config.add_route('some_route', '/some_url', factory='yourapp.security.SomeFactory')
```

https://ziggurat-foundations.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorial.html#pyramid-based-examples-of-permission-system-building  - like in this example.

The factory goal is to supply something like 
```python
self.__acl__ = [(Allow, Authenticated, u'view'), (Allow, 'robertlagrant', 'some_perm') ],
```

Ziggurat aids you so you don't have to write all the complicated queries to get results from db for different types of relations between users/groups/resources/permissions.

But you still need to create that __acl__ object on your own :-) If you supply context object and log in user then pyramids ACL policy will do its thing and either let you in or keep you out from specific view.

1. The `init_points` method has no connection to the training of your MLP, in fact it doesn't even know (or cares) that the function you're optimizing involves the training of another algorithm. The only reason for it to exist is to prime the Gaussian Process that effectively dictates the parameter search. So much so that one could argue that the only sensible choice for init points is 1.

2. `maximize` will attempt to find the parameter combination that maximizes the output of your function, whatever that is. While it is true that we look to maximums of the acquisition function in the process, this is only a proxy optimization to aid with the parameter search.

(mostly notes for myself incoming, so I don't forget anything).

If we go this route, we would need to allow ignore to be a single element or a list of elements, which cause them not to be verified here: https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/tokens.py#L101-L114. We need to be sure this wouldn't cause breaking changes for existing use cases either.

The new code paths we would need to be aware of (at a glance) would be:

* `fresh_token_required` raises a new exception (not KeyError) if it is called and the there is not `fresh` value in the token
* `refresh_token_required` raises a new exception if `type` is not in the token.
* If `type` is not in the token, assume that it is an access token and treat it as such here: https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/view_decorators.py#L208-L209
* what to do with `get_jwt_identity` if the identity claim is not present in the token? Probably just raise another new exception?

If we do this, it would make sense to allow these values to be absent when we are generating tokens as well, so we should add options to account for that too.

I'm not 100% convinced this would be a good idea to add to this extension yet, but it seems like it would be a good feature to have. I'll start playing with these changes when I have some free time, and go from there. It will probably take a bit to get these going, tested, etc.

You totally could, and there wouldn't be anything technically wrong with that, but I think that might not be the best idea, as you would then have multiple refresh tokens that are all still valid laying around.

For example, if you have the refresh tokens set to live for 7 days, and the access tokens set to live for 15 minutes, you may end up generating several new access and refresh tokens depending on how long the user is browsing your site. Every refresh token created still has the ability to create new access tokens for this user, which could in theory increase the chance of one of them being compromised.

For the sake of simplicity, I think it might be best to generate a single refresh token with a long expires time, and use that one repeatedly to generate new access tokens as they expire. But if you are worried about it, you could generate a new refresh token every time that you generate an access token, and then blacklist the just used refresh token to prevent it from being used again. I would need to add a method call for that, but it would be very simple to do (could have it done tomorrow). 

One potential downside to this is that if the call to refresh a token was interrupted part way through for any reason, the user would be stuck with an expired access token and revoked refresh token, and would be forced to sign in again. There may also be concerns with race conditions, where your front end fires off two asynchronous AJAX requests to refresh an access token at the same time. If the first request gets there first and revokes the refresh token, the second request could fail in a way that the front end wasn't expecting. You could absolutely make this work but it might take some addition consideration to get right, and I'm not sure if you would get significant security benefits by doing it.

If you specify a maximum Django version (like you have done in `setup.py`), you have to update this and push a release to PyPI every time Django releases.

Instead, just remove the maximum Django version requirement, and note the officially supported versions in PyPI and the `README.md`. That way users can upgrade Django if they want without breaking their project. You'll still have to update the CI configuration but you won't be preventing users from upgrading to newer Django versions in the interim.

You can also have Travis test current Django nightly builds while allowing failures for just those tests. That way you have advance notice of anything that breaks cacheback and can fix it before the official Django release.

Hmmm, weird. I could have sworn that I posted this comment last night, but apparently it didn't go through. I'll put it up just in case anyone wants to use the janky solution I discovered. Though you're right that a ManyToMany would be better here. I can't remember exactly why I decided not to do that in my initial implementation, which was basically the same as MultiSelectField, but less djangonic.

Further research has led me to a solution, but it's not pretty. :(

If I do this in the model code:
```
class LaxMultiSelectField(MultiSelectField):
    def validate(self, value, model_instance):
        pass

class MyModel(Model):
    my_field = LaxMultiSelectField(choices=[('placeholder', 'placeholder')], max_length=1024)
```
And this in the form code:
```
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(MyForm, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        self.fields['my_field'] = MultiSelectFormField(choices=[
            (section.machine_name, section.section_name)) for section in Section.objects.all()
        ])
```
It works. 

`CustomMultiSelectField` is required because django validates the chosen values against the choices specified in the class definition, which are just `'placeholder'`. Changing the choices in the form has no effect on this validation step, which is why I had to override `validate()` in the Field code.

Unfortunately, I'm not really sure how best to handle this in a way that would be appropriate for a PR. If you specify choices in the field definition, django throws validation errors. If you don't specify choices in the definition, django crashes because `self.choices` is empty during `Field.get_choices()`. And you can't specify choices as a queryset, because django will evaluate that queryset at import time, which is way too early.

Because of the way the date_range() function works is that it takes in the output of the schedule and converts to a range. Since the schedule() method always returns the open and close for each day, the date_range() function can only return frequency of 1D or less (this now checks that the frequency passed in is valid).

To solve your problem I added a new function convert_freq() that takes the output of the date_range() and converts to a lower frequency like 1W or 3D in your example. So it is a two-step process, make the date_range() with frequency=1D, then convert to the desired lower frequency. Perhaps in the future I will put it all into the date_range(), but for now this solves your problem.

Please see the test_date_range_lower_freq() in test_utils.py for an example.

However, the whole concept of ColanderAlchemy means there's a close coupling between the SqlAlchemy object and the end rendering in deform.  How myself (and at least one other person I know :wink: ) deals with limitations in ColanderAlchemy is to generate a colander schema with ColanderAlchemy and then modify the result afterwards to suit needs.  So, you'd call `SQLAlchemySchemaNode` on your class and exclude the JSON field (as that will cause an exception) and then modify the resulting schema into what you actually want.

Another solution is to use the `overrides` argument to `SQLAlchemySchemaNode` to specify a colander type.

The main problem is default ansible python module location. 
Default path is: `/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible`  when it installed from apt. 
But pip installing module to local libs: `/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible`

In my opinion it's huge problem and it must be resolved inside module. 

By the way, we can quickly hack it this using symlinks: 
```
ln -s /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible/modules/hashivault /usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible/modules/hashivault
```
```
ln -s /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible/module_utils/hashivault.py /usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible/module_utils/hashivault.py
```

I was too lazy to properly test the `/keys/upload` request but you get the idea. Also, my computer is pretty fast, but my network is too.  
The conclusion I draw from this is that we should make as few requests as possible, by generating as much one-time keys as possible.

This reasoning also answers your last point. We don't want to set keys_threshold to 1 because then we would do a lot of requests with very few one-time keys. Also, this code is not asynchronous like JS, and we want to block as rarely as possible.  
Let me know if you can think of a real-world scenario where having a low number of one-time keys on the HS is bad. As I see it, the worst case where we're just above the threshold, hence not triggering a new key generation, and more device than the HS has keys left want to talk to us for the first time at the exact same moment, thus not letting us replenish in time, will never happen under a normal load. Applications expecting huge traffic can set the threshold to something higher.

@Arkenklo - you are correct in thinking this is an error. I'll report this internally to the load-balancing team. To repeat your error; I can run this command:
```
$ cli4 --post expected_codes=2xx interval=15 retries=2 path=/status type=https /user/load_balancers/monitors
cli4: /user/load_balancers/monitors - 6007 json: cannot unmarshal string into Go value of type uint
$
```
However there's a feature in cli4 to set the JSON value in a POST as an numeric vs. a string. This uses **==** in the param passing. Using this will cause the command to work correctly.
```
$ cli4 --post expected_codes=2xx interval==15 retries==2 path=/status type=https /user/load_balancers/monitors
{
    "created_on": "2016-11-30T00:00:00.000000Z", 
    "description": "", 
    "expected": "", 
    "expected_codes": "2xx", 
    "follow_redirects": false, 
    "header": {}, 
    "id": "********************************", 
    "interval": 15, 
    "method": "GET", 
    "modified_on": "2016-11-30T00:00:00.000000Z", 
    "path": "/status", 
    "probe_timeout": 5, 
    "retries": 2, 
    "type": "https"
}
$
```
This is documented in the [cli4](https://github.com/cloudflare/python-cloudflare#cli) section of the main README. Repeated here:

> For API calls that need a set of date or parameters passed there is a item=value format. If you want a numeric value passed, then == can be used to force the value to be treated as a numeric value.

Please confirm this works for you.

@bf4 v1.10.0 release is around the corner and upgrade is easy.  Do you want to wait for that or build the fixed `deis-logspout` from master now?

If you have a development environment _already set up_, the steps would look something like this:

```
cd <local deis repo location>
git checkout master
git pull <remote name for github:deis/deis; e.g. upstream or origin depending on your setup> master
export DEV_REGISTRY=registry.hub.docker.com
cd logspout
make build deploy
```

The `build` task will build a new Docker image of `deis-logspout` based on master.

The `deploy` task will do a number of things.  It will push the newly built image up to a public Docker repository within your own account on Docker hub, it will update an etcd key in your cluster that instructs the `get_image` helper script on what Docker image to use for the `deis-logspout` component, and then it will stop and start the Fleet unit for `deis-logspout`.  Upon restart, the new image will automatically be pulled and used.

Let me know if that helps you or if you run into any problems with that procedure.

In general, it's difficult to say what a 'good' value of a metric is. This
depends hugely on the dataset you have. The best way of figuring out what a
good model is for your data is to try lots of different models and compare
them.

LightFM implements implicit feedback algorithms: passing in rating values
has no effect on the model. Fitting the same model repeatedly will give
slightly different results each time due to randomness inherent in the
fitting process.

1. This way of splitting the matrix is not going to work. The usual sklearn tools split row-wise: this will of course not work here, where the entire point is taking some interactions from every row and putting them in the test set. Have a look at this [blog post series](http://blog.ethanrosenthal.com/2016/11/07/implicit-mf-part-2/) to see an example of how to do the split.
2. Make sure your train and test matrices have the same dimensions. Having more users or items in the test set is nonsensical, as you would not have estimated representations for them in the training phase.

If you append your value to `report.extra` then it should create an assert and link to it from the HTML report. You shouldn't need to create a new column just for this new content:

```python
import pytest


@pytest.mark.hookwrapper
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call):
    pytest_html = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('html')
    outcome = yield
    report = outcome.get_result()
    extra = getattr(report, 'extra', [])
    if report.when == 'call':
        xfail = hasattr(report, 'wasxfail')
        if (report.skipped and xfail) or (report.failed and not xfail):
            extra.append(pytest_html.extras.text('content', 'CURL'))
        report.extra = extra
```

It isn't usual to set `__doc__` on instances of types, is it?

I believe so, because if you decided to store them as `state[name]` in `__getstate__`, you would have to pop them off the state in `__setstate__` so that the values didn't remain on the `__dict__` of the `HasTraits` instance and thus obstruct the descriptors using `__set__` and `__get__`.

So in the example you gave above, everything should pickle fine, but the reloaded `HasTraits` instance would no longer be using its `TraitTypes` because all the values exist on the instance's `__dict__`, so there would never be no check for descriptors on the class.

Ah, right so the behaviour is when proxy is reachable but remote host is not.

In that case, the timeout setting for proxy -> host connection is not configurable on our client side as that is a proxy server side connection made by the SSH _server_ on proxy host. Client side retries are not applicable either, proxy host may be doing that also.

ParallelSSH, and any client for that matter, can only affect its own connections - the proxy initiated connection is not one of them.

That timeout configuration can only be changed on the proxy host itself.

Eg:

```
In [2]: client = ParallelSSHClient(['11.213.132.3'], proxy_host='localhost', timeout=1, num_retries=1)

In [3]: now = datetime.now()
   ...: print(client.run_command('uname', stop_on_errors=False))
   ...: end = datetime.now()
   ...: print(end-now)
   ...: 
0:02:07.415910
```

Proxy host is `localhost` and reachable. Target host does not exist.

Changing localhost SSH settings to lower timeout changes this:

```
{'11.213.132.3': 
	host=11.213.132.3
	cmd=<Greenlet at 0x7f7423d8e730>
	channel=None
	stdout=None
	stderr=None
	stdin=None
	exception=("Error connecting to host '%s:%s' - %s", '11.213.132.3', 22, 'Connect failed')
}
0:00:01.211800
```

In short, will need to set an appropriate ssh config _on the proxy server_ for the desired timeout.

Hi! Turbodbc will automatically construct a connection string when you pass in keyword arguments ([see here](http://turbodbc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/getting_started.html#establish-a-connection-with-your-database)):

```
>>> from turbodbc import connect
>>> connection = connect(driver="PostgreSQL",
...                      server="hostname",
...                      port="5432",
...                      database="myDataBase",
...                      uid="myUsername",
...                      pwd="myPassword")
```

I guess this is what you are looking for? If you really want to have a function that creates a connection string and nothing else, you could use https://github.com/blue-yonder/turbodbc/blob/master/python/turbodbc/connect.py#L12, but this function is not part of the public interface and might change any second, though I would not expect it to.

If you try to build an SQLAlchemy dialect for some database, you might want to take a look at the Exasol dialect: https://github.com/blue-yonder/sqlalchemy_exasol/blob/master/sqlalchemy_exasol/turbodbc.py

Also, check out the list of SQLAlchemy dialects with turbodbc support before creating a new one, although I can't say anything about the quality/usage of these: http://turbodbc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/faq.html#can-i-use-turbodbc-together-with-sqlalchemy

Hi @PrashantRanjan09,

A standard approach to improve named entity recognition with continuous word representations is to use word embeddings, trained on large amount of unlabeled data, as feature in the NER system. Examples of this approach can be found in the following papers (in addition to the one from @sughodke's comment):
 - [Natural Language Processing (almost) from Scratch](http://www.jmlr.org/papers/volume12/collobert11a/collobert11a.pdf),
 - [Word representations: A simple and general method for semi-supervised learning](https://aclanthology.info/pdf/P/P10/P10-1040.pdf).

This general idea was previously explored with discrete word representation (based on word clustering) instead of word embeddings:
 - [Name Tagging with Word Clusters and Discriminative Training](http://www.aclweb.org/anthology/N04-1043),
 - [Semi-Supervised Learning for Natural Language](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/33296/62278990-MIT.pdf).

Best,
Edouard.

This highlights a larger issue with converting data coming out of a blend file. Previous versions of blender stored Smooth Threshold (smoothresh) as a short with whole numbers of degrees. More recent versions store it as a float in terms of radians. These are obviously incompatible.

What's needed is a way of providing a flag, based on the version of the file being imported, that controls the clamping and normalizing behaviour of the base-level data converters (to char, short, int, float, etc.).
